chapters.title: Rare Events Shape More of Life Than People Admit

summaryBullets: Treating rare high impact events as central rather than peripheral creates clearer judgment.
Many systems are shaped most by events that are hard to forecast in advance.
This matters because clarity improves when people stop assuming the future is mostly average.
A common mistake is building plans as if outliers barely matter.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the forecast looks stable until one extreme event rewrites the whole picture.
A practical starting move is to ask what outlier could dominate the result, reduce exposure to ruin, and avoid overconfidence in smooth averages.
keyTakeaways: Treating rare high impact events as central rather than peripheral creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: ask what outlier could dominate the result
reduce exposure to ruin
avoid overconfidence in smooth averages
plan for events bigger than the normal case

summaryBullets: Seeing how narrative fallacy creates neat explanations after the fact creates clearer judgment.
People tell cleaner stories after events than they could have predicted before them.
This matters because humility grows when explanations stop pretending the shock was obvious.
A common mistake is mistaking hindsight explanation for real foresight.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the event is suddenly described as inevitable only after it happened.
A practical starting move is to separate prediction from explanation, review what was visible beforehand, and treat tidy hindsight with suspicion.
keyTakeaways: Seeing how narrative fallacy creates neat explanations after the fact creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: separate prediction from explanation
review what was visible beforehand
treat tidy hindsight with suspicion
write uncertainty before outcomes arrive
